conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

Are you currently responsible for Alex? By this I mean, does Alex live with you, or are you the main person contributing to their upbringing and care costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't live with me, but I do help out financially when I can - I contribute towards things like clothes and other bits and bobs for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I need to understand this a bit better.

When you say Alex doesn't live with you, who does Alex live with? And would you say you are the main person financially responsible for Alex, or is someone else (such as the other parent or another person) the primary person responsible for Alex's care and upbringing?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with my ex-partner. I wouldn't say I'm the main person financially responsible - my ex is really the one who has the day-to-day care and covers most of the costs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. 

Just to confirm - is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or do you know if they are receiving it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, my ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 8 years old and lives with your ex-partner
- Your ex-partner has day-to-day care of Alex and covers most of the costs
- Your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- You contribute financially when you can, but you're not the main person responsible

Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. The benefit is typically awarded to the person who is primarily responsible for the child - usually the person the child lives with. Since your ex-partner has day-to-day care of Alex and is already claiming Child Benefit for them, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

This is because Child Benefit can only be claimed by one person for each child, and it goes to the person who is primarily responsible for the child - typically the person they live with and who provides their day-to-day care. Since your ex-partner has Alex living with them, provides the main care, and is already claiming Child Benefit, you cannot also claim it.

However, the financial support you provide for Alex is still valuable and important for their wellbeing, even though it doesn't qualify you for Child Benefit in this situation.

Is there anything else I can help clarify?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16, so meets the age requirement). However, Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner who has day-to-day care and covers most costs. The claimant contributes financially when possible but is not the main person responsible for Alex. Additionally, the ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim for a child, and it goes to the person primarily responsible for the child - typically the person they live with. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives with the claimant's ex-partner. The ex-partner has primary care responsibility and is already claiming Child Benefit. As only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the ex-partner is the primary carer, the claimant is not eligible.

Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim for a child, and it goes to the person primarily responsible for the child - typically the person they live with. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives with the claimant's ex-partner. The ex-partner has primary care responsibility and is already claiming Child Benefit. As only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the ex-partner is the primary carer, the claimant is not eligible.
